section.title: An Invitation to the Thirsty
section.sectionRationale: Fresh from the Servant's atoning death and Zion's promised fruitfulness, Isaiah opens the widest door in the whole book: come, everyone who thirsts, come, buy without money. It is the natural next word after chapter 53, the invitation that flows from a finished, costly atonement, offered free to all who will come.
section.thesis: Because the Servant's suffering has secured salvation, God now issues an open, urgent invitation to everyone who thirsts to come without money and be satisfied, to seek Him while He may be found, and to trust that His word, once sent out, will not return empty but will accomplish everything He intends.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the free invitation to eat and drink; the call to seek the LORD and return to Him, since His thoughts are not our thoughts; and the sure, fruitful power of God's word, sending His people out with joy.

units.label: Come, all you who are thirsty
units.note: buy without money and without cost

verses.text: “Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters; and you without money, come, buy, and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without cost!
Why spend money on that which is not bread, and your labor on that which does not satisfy? Listen carefully to Me, and eat what is good, and your soul will delight in the richest of foods.
Incline your ear and come to Me; listen, so that your soul may live. I will make with you an everlasting covenant, My {{chesedDavid|loving devotion}} promised to David.
Behold, I have made him a witness to the nations, a leader and commander of the peoples.
Surely you will summon a nation you do not know, and nations who do not know you will run to you. For the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, has bestowed glory on you.”

units.label: Seek the LORD while He may be found
units.note: His thoughts are not our thoughts

verses.text: Seek the LORD while He may be found; call on Him while He is near.
Let the wicked man forsake his own way and the unrighteous man his own thoughts; let him return to the LORD, that He may have compassion, and to our God, for He will freely pardon.
“For My thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways My ways,” declares the LORD.
“For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so My ways are higher than your ways and My thoughts than your thoughts.

units.label: My word will not return empty

verses.text: For just as rain and snow fall from heaven and do not return without watering the earth, making it bud and sprout, and providing seed to sow and food to eat,
so My {{dabar|word}} that proceeds from My mouth will not return to Me empty, but it will accomplish what I please, and it will prosper where I send it.
You will indeed go out with joy and be led forth in peace; the mountains and hills will burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field will clap their hands.
Instead of the thornbush, a cypress will grow, and instead of the brier, a myrtle will spring up; they will make a name for the LORD, an everlasting sign, never to be destroyed.”

chesedDavid.gloss: The loving devotion, or sure mercies, promised to David, referring to God's covenant faithfulness to David's line in 2 Samuel 7. Paul quotes this exact phrase in Acts 13:34 as proof that Jesus, raised from the dead, is the fulfillment of that everlasting Davidic promise.

dabar.gloss: Word, spoken utterance with inherent power to act. God's dabar does not merely describe reality, it accomplishes it, the same creative word that spoke light into being in Genesis 1 and that John identifies as the eternal Word who became flesh, John 1:1, 14.

basic.context: Right after the Suffering Servant's atonement and Zion's promised fruitfulness, Isaiah throws open an invitation to everyone who thirsts, no money required, to come and be satisfied. It reads like the natural next word once the cost of salvation has already been paid by another.
basic.hermeneutics: This is gospel invitation in Old Testament dress, urgent, universal in its call though answered by those who actually come, and grounded not in human effort, buy without money, but in God's initiative and provision. The chapter also confronts the difference between God's ways and ours, and promises that His spoken word never fails to accomplish its purpose.
basic.originalLanguages: God's chasdei David, the sure loving devotion promised to David, becomes, in Acts 13:34, Paul's proof text that the resurrection of Jesus fulfills God's everlasting covenant with David's line. And God's dabar, word, is pictured as rain and snow, never returning empty, always accomplishing what it is sent to do.
basic.typology: The free invitation to water, bread, wine, and milk without cost anticipates Jesus' own invitation, if anyone thirsts, let him come to Me and drink, John 7:37, and the free gift of the water of life in Revelation 22:17. The everlasting covenant and mercies of David point directly to Christ, David's greater Son.
basic.christ: Jesus stands in the temple and echoes this very chapter, if anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink, John 7:37, offering Himself as the fulfillment of Isaiah's free invitation. Paul, in his first recorded sermon, quotes Isaiah 55:3 to prove that Jesus' resurrection fulfills the sure, everlasting mercies promised to David, Acts 13:34.
basic.god: God is not a reluctant seller extracting payment, He is a Host who has already paid the cost and now simply calls the thirsty to come and receive freely. His ways are higher than ours precisely in this: where we calculate what people deserve, He freely pardons all who return to Him.
basic.crossReferences: John 7:37 has Jesus issue the same invitation to the thirsty at the Feast of Tabernacles. Acts 13:34 quotes 55:3 of Christ's resurrection fulfilling David's sure mercies. Revelation 22:17 closes the whole Bible with the same free invitation, let the one who is thirsty come.
basic.oneStory: From a costly, finished atonement flows a free, urgent invitation: come, thirsty ones, without money, and be satisfied. That invitation runs through Jesus' own words at the temple and through the last page of Revelation, because the whole Bible's story ends exactly where Isaiah 55 begins, an open door to anyone who will come.

context.literary: A hinge chapter, moving from the Servant's atonement and Zion's restoration into direct, urgent evangelistic appeal, structured as a marketplace cry, come, buy, eat, followed by a call to seek and return, and closing with an oracle on the unstoppable power of God's word.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles who had spent themselves on Babylon's false securities; Isaiah calls them to stop laboring for what does not satisfy and instead receive, freely, the covenant blessings God had promised through David, now extended to nations that did not previously know Israel's God.
context.canonical: Echoed by Jesus' invitation at the Feast of Tabernacles, John 7:37, cited by Paul as proof of the resurrection fulfilling David's covenant, Acts 13:34, and echoed in the Bible's final invitation, Revelation 22:17, making this one of Scripture's clearest threads of gospel continuity from Old Testament to New.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people, having just heard of the Servant's atonement, to respond with active faith, coming, buying, eating, seeking, and returning, and to assure them that God's purposes, once spoken, cannot fail to accomplish what He intends.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is largely direct address and command, come, seek, call, return, making it prescriptive in the fullest sense; it calls for an active response of faith rather than passive reflection alone.

debates.question: Does 'buy without money' contradict the idea that faith still requires a response?

views.label: It emphasizes grace alone
views.case: The image stresses that salvation cannot be earned or purchased by human effort or wealth, since it has already been paid for by another, matching the free grace of the gospel, Ephesians 2:8-9.

views.label: It still calls for a real, costly response
views.case: Coming, buying, listening, and returning are all active verbs; grace is free in its cost to us but still requires our whole-hearted turning, seek the LORD, return to the LORD, verses 6-7.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: salvation is entirely free because another has paid its price, yet it must be actively received, by coming, listening, and turning, exactly as saving faith always is in Scripture.

study.originalLanguages: God's chasdei David, sure covenant mercies to David, ground the invitation in a real, historical promise that Paul later ties directly to the resurrection of Christ. And God's dabar, word, is likened to rain and snow that never fall in vain, always accomplishing the purpose for which it was sent.

typology.type: The free invitation to water, wine, and milk without cost, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' invitation to the thirsty at the temple, John 7:37, and the final invitation of Revelation 22:17.

typology.type: The everlasting covenant, the sure mercies of David, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Paul's proof from Christ's resurrection that God's covenant with David is fulfilled in Jesus, Acts 13:34.

typology.type: The word that does not return empty, verse 11
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the living Word who accomplishes the Father's will perfectly, John 1:1, 14, and the gospel word that never returns void when it is proclaimed.

study.christ: Jesus deliberately echoes this chapter when He stands and cries out at the temple, if anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink, John 7:37, presenting Himself as the fulfillment of Isaiah's free invitation. Paul, preaching his first recorded sermon in Acts 13, quotes Isaiah 55:3 directly, the sure mercies of David, as proof that Jesus' resurrection is the fulfillment of God's everlasting covenant with David's line. And the dabar, the word that never returns empty, finds its fullest sense in Christ Himself, the Word who was with God and was God, and whose gospel word still accomplishes exactly what the Father sends it to do.
study.god: God is generous beyond calculation, offering the richest of foods to those who have no money, and He is also unfathomable, His thoughts higher than our thoughts as the heavens are higher than the earth, meaning His mercy toward the wicked who return to Him will always exceed what human logic expects.

crossReferences.note: Jesus echoes this chapter's invitation, if anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink.
Paul quotes Isaiah 55:3 of Christ's resurrection fulfilling the sure mercies of David.
The Bible's closing invitation, let the one who is thirsty come, echoes Isaiah 55:1.
Salvation as gift, not earned, matching the buy without money picture of this chapter.

study.oneStory: An invitation this wide, come, all who thirst, buy without money, only makes sense once someone else has already paid the cost, which is exactly what chapter 53 has just described. From there the whole Bible keeps issuing the same call, through Jesus at the temple, through the apostles' preaching, to the last page of Revelation, where the thirsty are still being invited to come.

sermon.title: The Great Invitation: Come! Drink! Eat! Live!
sermon.why: Preached by John Piper on July 31, 1988 from Isaiah 55:1-3, opening a five-part series through the whole chapter, this message unpacks the free, urgent invitation to come and be satisfied in God rather than laboring for what does not satisfy.

quiz.q: What does the picture of buying wine and milk without money teach about salvation?

options.text: Salvation is offered freely because its cost has already been paid by another; it cannot be earned by human effort or wealth.
options.note: This matches the free grace of the gospel, Ephesians 2:8-9.

options.text: It teaches that literal wine and milk are the only true satisfaction.
options.note: The wine and milk are figures for the richest, most satisfying provision God gives, not literal beverages alone.

options.text: It means God requires no response at all from those who come.
options.note: Salvation's cost is entirely free, but coming, buying, listening, and seeking are still active responses called for throughout the chapter.

options.text: It shows that only the wealthy can receive God's blessings.
options.note: The invitation is explicitly for those without money, the opposite of wealth-based access.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaiah 55:3 in Acts 13:34?

options.text: He quotes the sure mercies of David as proof that Christ's resurrection fulfills God's everlasting covenant with David.
options.note: Paul's argument in Acts 13 ties this verse directly to the resurrection of Jesus.

options.text: He uses it to argue that David's line ended with the exile.
options.note: Paul argues the opposite, that God's promise to David continues and is fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: He never references Isaiah 55 in his sermons.
He applies it only to Gentile believers, excluding Jewish hearers.
options.note: The sermon does reach Gentile God-fearers present, but Acts 13 is addressed first to a Jewish synagogue audience, not exclusively Gentile.

quiz.q: What does verse 11 promise about God's word?

options.text: It will never return to Him empty; it will always accomplish what He intends and prosper where He sends it.
options.note: The rain and snow image in verses 10-11 illustrates the certain effectiveness of God's spoken word.

options.text: It sometimes fails to accomplish its purpose.
options.note: The verse explicitly denies this; the word never returns empty.

options.text: It refers only to written Scripture, not spoken proclamation.
options.note: Written Scripture is included, but the image of rain and snow suggests active, sent-out speech, encompassing spoken proclamation as well.

options.text: It means God's word changes depending on human approval.
options.note: The word's success is independent of human approval; it accomplishes what God pleases.

quiz.q: What is the point of contrasting God's thoughts and ways with ours in verses 8-9?

options.text: God's mercy toward the wicked who return to Him will always exceed human expectation or logic.
options.note: This follows directly from verse 7's promise that God will freely pardon.

options.text: It teaches that human reasoning and God's reasoning are basically identical.
options.note: The verses explicitly say the opposite, God's ways are higher.

options.text: It means humans can never understand anything about God.
options.note: God's ways do exceed ours, but the passage still calls people to seek, listen, and understand; it is not total unknowability.

options.text: It refers only to differences in language and vocabulary.
options.note: It is about moral and redemptive logic, mercy exceeding expectation, not vocabulary.

questions: The invitation is to those who are thirsty and have no money. What are you laboring for right now that does not satisfy, and what would it look like to stop and simply come?
Verse 6 says seek the LORD while He may be found. Is there an urgency to your own pursuit of God, or have you let it drift into someday?
Verse 7 promises God will freely pardon those who forsake their own way and return to Him. What way of your own do you need to forsake this week?
God's thoughts are higher than our thoughts, especially in mercy. Where have you assumed God's forgiveness has limits that His own word does not actually place on it?
The chapter ends with thorns replaced by cypress, briers by myrtle, a picture of transformed landscape. What area of your life feels thorny right now that you can trust God's word to transform?

translationNotes.note: Sure mercies of David, chasdei David, is quoted in the Greek Old Testament and directly by Paul in Acts 13:34 as ta hosia Dauid, the holy and sure things of David, linking the phrase to Christ's resurrection.
Without money and without cost intensifies the same idea twice in Hebrew for emphasis; the invitation is doubly free, not merely discounted.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 55:1-13; no em dashes present in this passage. Sermon verified this session at desiringgod.org.
